reviews.review_text: Mildly enjoyable - it's ok nothing special - characters are bland, boring and predictable - nowhere near as captivating as L4D1 or L4D2 couldn't find any magic to grab me - don't like all this satanistic look of the SI's in games there's too much of that crap these days. I'd play doom if i wanted that sort of stuff.

It's no Left 4 Dead and it shouldn't have been marketed as a successor to it but as it's own thing it's a fun game, especially on the higher difficulties. Every game plays differently due to corruption cards and also player playstyles depending on how they setup their decks (Melee, high DPS, Shotgun.etc)

Definitely worth picking up when on sale, still has a player base too with crossplay being an option.
Basically Luxury Left 4 Dead with character builds and similar story, smooth gameplay and well designed boss fights.
Not as good as Left 4 Dead but still a fun game if you just want mindless zombie shooting.

My favorite part about this game is that the players are all chill. Everyone is just "Yeah L4D2 is more fun but I'd rather not deal with the community. " It's not even a bad game, it's just that it was hyped to be the next L4D. Multiplayer vs isn't worth the effort though since nobody plays it.

Super fun gameplay loop. Maps look good and variable, especially in the expansions. Character abilities and cards are interesting. I'm not normally into deck building but found it fun to build and experiment with decks. Feels like a brand new game with different decks.

6/10
I’m gonna be honest: if this game wasn’t constantly marketed as the “spiritual successor” to Left 4 Dead, I probably would’ve been much less harsh on it.

But that’s exactly the problem.

You CANNOT spend years telling people that Back 4 Blood is the successor to Left 4 Dead and that it’s made by the creators of Left 4 Dead, then deliver something that completely misses what made those games special in the first place.

Is it a terrible game?
No.

Is it Left 4 Dead 3?
Absolutely not.

The story is okay. The Cleaners are decent enough, the weapons feel good, and there are moments where you can see some genuinely cool ideas buried under everything else.

But man… the vibe just isn’t there.

Left 4 Dead 1 and 2 had this unique atmosphere that made every campaign memorable. The music, the characters, the pacing, the special infected, the simplicity of jumping in with friends and immediately having fun.

Back 4 Blood feels like it looked at Left 4 Dead and said:

“What if we added 37 systems, 12 currencies, card decks, progression trees and a bunch of DLCs?”

Instead of improving the formula, it feels like it overcomplicated it.

And speaking of DLCs…

There are WAY too many paid expansions for a game that never convinced me it deserved them in the first place.

Every time I played, I couldn’t stop comparing it to Left 4 Dead 2, and every single time it made me want to go play Left 4 Dead 2 instead.

That’s probably the biggest criticism I can give.

Not because Back 4 Blood is awful.

But because after a few hours I realized I’d rather replay a 2009 game than continue playing the supposed successor released more than a decade later.

Overall:

Good guns.
Decent story.
Some fun moments.

But as a “spiritual successor” to Left 4 Dead?

Massive disappointment.

It’s not a bad game.

It’s just painfully average living in the shadow of two masterpieces.
This game could've been a solid choice for L4D2 alternatives out there. It is inferior to L4D2 sure, but it was refreshing for me. Sadly developers seem to gave up on this.

Ok, so you've read the other reviews and now you know Back 4 Blood is a great game, but I want to talk about something different: playing it with a gyro controller.

What's a gyro controller?  Well, that's any controller that allows you to control how you look and aim simply by moving the controller itself.

See, I'm the kinda guy who sucks at aiming with controllers, but I also like to sit back and chill with a controller.  And if a game can be adequately configured to support a usable gyro then it immediately gets my attention.  And the easier it is to configure, the more consistent and fluid it feels, and the more transparent it is, well...  That's where I'll put in the effort to help others sit up and notice.

Back 4 Blood, without a doubt, ticks every gyro-gamer box because it's in-game controller configuration let's us COMPLETELY turn off any joystick-shaping features like acceleration, aim assist and deadzone, so that we can truly map the gyro to look and aim without any of the quirks we often see in other games that CLAIM to disable such shaping but actually leave a little something enabled under the hood that may utterly destroy tracking consistency and muscle memory.

Now yes - I know - you simply can't beat binding a gyro to the actual mouse, but when a game just gets out of the way and let's you bind a gyro that "feels" like a mouse via "Gyro To Joystick Camera" without the compromises doing so often brings (stutter cause by mixed input, flickering glyphs, etc) then it very much deserves to be highlighted.  And Back 4 Blood absolutely stands out where so many other games promise to "get out of the way" but somehow ultimately fail.

"Gyro To Joystick Camera" is truly impressive in Back 4 Blood.

Anyway, I have 8BitDo Ultimate 2 Wireless, Steam Controller (2026) and Steam Deck pure gamepad controller layouts available for Back 4 Blood in Steam's "Community Layouts", but here are the Controller settings you will need to change in-game to achieve a consistent, fluid experience:

Horizontal Sensitivity: 100
Vertical Sensitivity: 100
Right Stick Deadzone: 0 (ZERO)

Camera Movement: LINEAR
Camera Acceleration: OFF
Camera Smooth Duration: 0 (ZERO)

Aim Assist: OFF

Aim Down Sights Low Zoom Sensitivity Multiplier: 1
Aim Down Sights High Zoom Sensitivity Multiplier: 1

Here is the "Gyro To Joystick Camera" Steam controller configuration settings that offer a good starting point:

Minimum Gyro Input Speed: 0 (ZERO)
Maximum Gyro Input Speed: 720

Gyro Sensitivity: 5x
Gyro Speed Deadzone: 0.1
Gyro Precision Speed: 10

All other setting are kept at defaults; I'll leave latching the gyro to your own personal preference.  For ADS, we can configure a MODE SHIFT for the gyro with a LEFT TRIGGER activator, where the MODE SHIFT gyro is configured as above but with these changes:

Gyro Sensitivity: 2.5x
Gyro Precision Speed: 5

And, finally, here are the Steam controller configuration settings I use to help tame the right Joystick, since we have set the in-game joystick sensitivity to extremely high:

Horizontal Scale: 60
Vertical Scale: 40
Stick Response Curve: EXTRA WIDE

I find that this configuration presents an exceptional gyro experience, but don't be afraid to make it your own through experimentation and tuning.

Enjoy!

Take left for dead 2 and remove all the humor, the good character interactions, the quality level and enemy design and replace it with cosmetics and weapon details.
now remove all the satisfying gameplay elements like shooting with actual feedback and good sound design.

thats what Back 4 Blood is. Its proof that saying "From the guys who brought you X and Y" doesn't actually mean ♥♥♥♥

Imagine playing in the community servers for L4D2-

There's perk systems, custom abilities, modifiers to every stage, every enemy, there's roles you can select and weapons you can upgrade. 

Back 4 Blood is a lot like that, if all the community-made content was basegame. And over the years, it's gotten even more feature bloat so now it currently feels like you're playing in a community server for B4B. 

 - - - 

This game is... good. But overall isn't worth your time.

It has some cool ideas, it's got a lot of mechanics you can sink your teeth into, and most every run feels unique with a lot of variables that you can master over time. That also becomes the detriment. There's too much of everything and doesn't make for a casual experience until you've done it for a few hours or hundreds of hours. The game wants you to explore different playstyles and decks, but it forces you to grind for SEVERAL campaigns to be able to try them. There's a trailer showing different starter decks for example, but you only get one of them with 5 basic cards inside. This is the level of care the devs have. Well-meaning, and there IS a lot of hard work put into this. But it does ultimately miss the mark and there's a big divide between what the players enjoy, and what the devs want this game to be. 

- - - 
THE GOOD:

The 8 base characters have a lot of dialog, they DO have perks and abilities, but they're kept open enough so that you don't get pigeon-holed into playing a specific playstyle. That's a hard balance to have and it's actually done really well here! Jim can be a sniper with his perk, but he can just as easily go wild with an SMG or wreck mutations with a shotgun. You're not forced to play a sniper, nor use a sniper rifle. That openness is pretty good. 

Decks are interesting. A whole bunch of effects anywhere from stat boosts, situational buffs, new abilities, and even things for money hoarding and economy. A lot of the fun is putting together a deck and actually trying it out. Seeing if the theory lines up with practice. You are limited to about 8 decks, and you can't read other player's decks. 

Min-maxing weapon attachments. Every gun has about 4 attachment slots, there's about 4 types per slot, with 4 rarities. You can have a precision shotgun that lets you swap easily, or a speed shotgun all about reloading fast and moving fast. There's satisfaction in figuring out when's the right time to switch weapons, and if it's worth losing or trading your attachments over. Fun! Downside being, it WILL slow down your run if you or someone else is taking too long to minmax. 

Teamwork is nice. Being able to ping stuff and mantle around feels great after coming from L4D. You can divide up loot, share ammo, share money, and work to cover each other's weaknesses. If you want a game where you and a buddy (or more) struggle against difficult odds, you might have a good time here. 

- - - 
THE BAD: 

Horde vs Mutations. The horde is vast and smart, same as L4D. They surround you, slow you, and you'll always need to be able to cut your way through a crowd to get to where you need to go. The mutations (special infected) are familiar enough but much tankier. They need focus fire, and also to be hit in their weakspots. These are two separate problems to face, and as the game gets harder as you progress, it becomes increasingly frustrating and dissatisfying. 
Your SMG will work great against the horde, but you take damage from mutations because you can't kill them fast enough. 
Your shotgun is great against the mutations, but it's awkward and clumsy working your way through a horde. 
Teamwork naturally helps, but you will always be in a situation where you need to build for both situations. By the time you find an answer, you're left with what feels like an incomplete build.

Build limits. If your deck does not have Two Is One, One Is None (equip a second primary gun) and Admin Reload (auto-reload your gun when not in use), you are worse off. These two cards are so useful, and helps to solve the problem with horde vs mutations, that any deck you build will be lesser without it. But you also need at least one economy card to help buy upgrades. Your sniper deck is useless because the game is not designed for you to be trailing behind. Your melee deck faces trouble with ranged mutations and bosses and DoT effects like burning and acid. Your SMG deck feels terrible against mutations while your shotgun deck lacks range, and everyone is running assault rifles so you will run out of ammo if you run that deck. You start to feel so close to something powerful and good, but it never quite reaches there. 

Unfair Corruptions. Corruption cards are added at the start of every level, they decide the difficulty level of enemies, and special conditions. Some of these corruptions are light changes, like having draining health you gain back by killing enemies. Some are huge interrupts like having pangs of hunger, or very small but regenerating healthbars, or horde enemies that give you a permanent debuff for the level. It does keep things fresh and interesting, but it's not fun to lose a run because of some BS new rules that get in the way of your build or playstyle. 

No mod support. The devs explicitly did not want to deal with mods or community creations, even giving a cease-and-desist to a team that made their own custom levels. There are many small changes that would make this game better, bugfixes and new features, but they'll never come to be. This game can be something great if given the tools, but we are instead stuck in a game that loves to shoot itself in the foot. 

The GRIND. It takes a full campaign on the starting difficult to unlock roughly 5-7 cards if I remember right. This is also sifting through the item packages that mix in sprays and cosmetics, making it harder to get to the specific cards you're looking for. The decks are this game's best selling point, and they put it behind a timesink! That's terrible! The trailer literally shows example decks, and this would be great for starting out and learning what cards you would want to unlock and what decks you might like to put together. But the example decks don't exist, and any friends you want to share this experience with will have to put up with a feeling of dissatisfaction they'll be feeling for the rest of their hours on this game. Terrible. 

- - - 
THE END:

If this game had mod support, it'd be a generational classic. But as it is, it doesn't respect your time, doesn't value your creativity, asks of you to spend hours and hours grinding just to get to the starting point of what the game is about, and still frequently has bugs and issues that will never get fixed. I wouldn't recommend buying this game unless it's on sale. If you want something you can drop into your friends with, you're better off picking something else. If you're autistic and love the idea of minmaxing your build and your weapons and constantly coming up with deck ideas and tweaks (like me!), then maybe give this a try. Just don't play with console players, they have no idea what they're doing. 

- - - 
Versus?

There's no Versus mode. Supposedly they couldn't do it legally, so they came up with Swarm mode instead. In Swarm you take turns playing as either the survivors or the infected, in a small area in a random map. Whichever team survives the longest as the survivors, wins. It's actually pretty interesting and cool! Unfortunately, there were game-breaking bugs with the mode for LITERAL MONTHS. It was so awful, everyone gave up playing it. Which meant you would be waiting in the lobby for half an hour, just to start a match where someone leaves immediately and the game ends. And even if you did play, you'd get a bug where you were already dead so your time was wasted. This could've been a great mode if the devs had put more care into it, it's a shame.
There's too much going on with this game, super confusing, would not recommend. I really tried to give this one a couple of runs, but I can't bring myself to like this

Back 4 Blood is a fun co-op zombie shooter that is best when played with friends. The game focuses on teamwork as you fight through waves of infected enemies, complete objectives, collect supplies, and try to survive each level. It feels similar to classic co-op zombie games, but adds its own systems like character abilities, weapon attachments, and card-based builds.

One of the best parts of Back 4 Blood is the co-op gameplay. Each player can choose a different Cleaner with unique perks, which makes team composition matter. The card system also lets you customize your playstyle, whether you want more damage, better healing, extra stamina, or stronger support abilities. This gives the game more variety than just running and shooting.

The combat is fast and chaotic, especially when special infected show up or when a horde rushes the team. Weapons feel solid, and upgrading your loadout during a run makes progression feel rewarding. The game is especially enjoyable when everyone communicates and works together because splitting up or wasting resources can quickly get the whole team in trouble.

However, Back 4 Blood can feel repetitive after a while. Many missions follow the same basic pattern of moving through an area, fighting hordes, and reaching a safe room. It can also be frustrating with random teammates if people do not cooperate. Some players may also feel that it does not fully capture the same magic as older zombie co-op games.

Overall, Back 4 Blood is a solid zombie shooter with fun combat, good co-op moments, and enough customization to keep things interesting. It is not perfect, but it is worth playing if you enjoy team-based survival games and fighting through huge groups of infected.

Rating: 7.5/10
this game not so good in world of gamers so better play LEFT 4 DEAD 2 WITH MODS (THE NAUGHTY KIND)
This is not L4D3, but I enjoyed it for what it is. Fun coop zombie horde shooter. Decent soundtrack too. Bought it on a deep sale with all the addons.

As a fan of zombie games, I was eager to dive into *Back 4 Blood*, and it did not disappoint! This cooperative first-person shooter revitalizes the genre with its fast-paced action and thrilling gameplay. The game's premise takes inspiration from the beloved *Left 4 Dead* series, allowing players to team up and battle swarms of terrifying undead.

One of the standout features of *Back 4 Blood* is its character system. Each character has unique abilities and backstories that add depth to the gameplay. I enjoyed experimenting with different characters to see how their skills complemented my teammates’ playstyles. The game promotes teamwork, and coordinating with friends makes for a rewarding experience.

The card system also adds an interesting layer to the gameplay. You can choose cards to customize your character's abilities and the challenges you'll face in each run. This element keeps the game fresh and encourages replayability, as no two sessions feel exactly the same.

The graphics and sound design are impressive, creating an immersive atmosphere filled with tension and excitement. The environments are varied and filled with details that enhance the overall experience. From deserted towns to dark forests, there's always something new to explore.

While I loved *Back 4 Blood*, I recommend giving it a try to see if it resonates with you as it did with me. The game can be chaotic and may take some time to master, but if you enjoy cooperative gameplay with friends and a good dose of zombie-slaying action, it's definitely worth checking out!
Gunplay feels generic, gameplay is boring, gun selection is fine, the introduction sucks. Music is great. I would rather play a modern COD than this. That says a lot.

Solo, this game is kinda sluggish and boring but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ around with some buddies makes the whole experience 10x better. 

The "Roguelike" nature of the card system honestly kept me more intrigued than I thought it would. It's nothing revolutionary but if you structure your deck even in a half-assed attempt, it can be a rewarding system to mess with.

Graphics are kinda mid for 2021 standards. Everything looks like plastic-y and smooth, which for a zombie horde massacre game, kinda takes away from the experience imo. There's a mod to disable the HUD and I made a personal ReShade preset and my enjoyment at the very least doubled.

I'd honestly recommend picking this game up only if it goes on sale cause who in their right mind is paying $60 for this. It's a fun game if you wanna just turn your brain off and blast through some zombies, just don't come in expecting L4D3.

Some people might really like the approach to difficulty and progression in this game, but my friends and I disliked it- after giving it multiple tries. Having progression is a nice idea but not necessary for a game like this, especially one that is based on grinding. Similarly, a high level of difficulty that drives a detailed "meta" discussion on technique and style is ok. But, there are probably a few too many variables and too much of an investment is needed in order to really develop valid expertise in an enjoyable way. I think the game would have benefited from being a bit more L4D-like in terms of simplicity, keeping but refining the general gameplay, adding a mechanic or source of difficulty here or there- but not going for the triple A Call of Duty, uber-competitive Counterstrike, or 'add all the features that any successful game ever included approach. It's too much to ask in terms of balancing on the part of the developers, and acclimating and developing expertise on the part of the players. The best analogy I can come up with is like if MarioKart included the ability to craft, buy a house, and required you to level up all the players and get a good card pull to routinely enjoy yourself- and also got rid of the ability to just pick up and enjoy the game with friends. I'm late to the review, but wanted to share my thoughts in case future games are based on this one. Great previous work! Great promise! But, really not an enjoyable game for me.

Its an alright game, but i'm pretty confident the devs abandoned it (last patch was in 2024), which makes it not worth it, especially not full price, but dare I say not even on sale for like 3$. Keep your money. Play L4D instead.

I don't have many words for this game man, It feels like a slog to even write this review. 

It gets boring really fast even if you have a dedicated party. I’m currently sitting at the start of act 2 and I honestly don’t have the willpower to push any further. Does that mean that the game and it's mechanics are bad? Not at all. The weapon play and movement are very fluid, the animations for the guns are pretty nice sometimes, the recoil feels good, the melee... passes. I just think the problem is that after about 20 minutes reality starts to set in and you’re sat there thinking, like “why the ♥♥♥♥ am I playing this?”. I picked it up for like 5$. I'm not that pressed over five bucks. But it being sold for 60$ at one point. That's insane.

Honestly the worst thing about games like this is that they're mediocre. Not bad. just OKAY. And you can waste your time on OKAY. You shouldn't, but you can. You know what you can't waste time on? You can't waste time on something that's just bad. And I think this game has made me appreciate bad games.
Gameplay gets old fast, characters are annoying and look weird, story is laughable.
This is one of the best PvE zombie shooters out there. Ignore the PvP mode and the overdramatic hate. The game has come a long way since the beta.

Going into this game, I was hoping for the same Left 4 Dead experience. What I got was kinda' like Left 4 dead, but without some of my favorite things. I miss the end screen telling me and my friend who got the most kills, who scared the witch or even how good your accuracy was. I believe they were trying to make it a more friendly experience for the single player. When the game first came out, I would have given this game a not so great review- but now that all the expansions are out- it seems like a very flushed out game.  I wanted more from this game, but it seemed to come to an abrupt halt. Its a great game if you wanna pick it up to play with friends.
